title: Excuses for Not Praying
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
series: Lessons from the School of Prayer
source_files: Lessons from the School of Prayer 9 - Excuses for Not Praying - Matthew 10_38-42 - Elder Anderson.txt

outline.title: Excuse 1: I Am Too Busy to Pray
subpoints: Martin Luther: the busier I am, the more I must pray
Mary and Martha: Jesus says Mary chose the better part (Luke 10:38-42)
We fill every quiet moment with distraction rather than reflection or prayer
Running on empty — the man who refused to stop for gas illustrates daily prayerlessness

outline.title: Excuse 2: I Feel Too Spiritually Dry to Pray
subpoints: The assumption that approach to God depends on personal feelings dishonors Christ’s mediation
We cast a slur on Christ when mood becomes the deciding factor for obedience
God commands persistent prayer regardless of emotional state — the persistent widow (Luke 18)
The friend at midnight: ask, seek, knock — those who keep asking are the ones who receive

outline.title: Excuse 3: I Feel No Need to Pray
subpoints: Self-confidence can masquerade as spiritual maturity and crowd out prayer
Joshua’s failure with the Gibeonites: they did not ask counsel of the Lord (Joshua 9:14-15)
Hezekiah’s pride before the Babylonian envoys: God withdrew from him to test him (2 Chron. 32:31)
The worst thing God can do to a prayerless person is nothing — simply let them continue

outline.title: Excuse 4: I Am Too Bitter to Pray
subpoints: Bitterness at first makes prayer cold and formulaic, then stops it entirely
Real prayer forces us to acknowledge our own sin and confess it — bitterness prevents this
Forgiveness of others demonstrates sincerity in our own desire to be forgiven (Matthew 6:14-15)
Harboring bitterness while pretending to pray is like a politician making empty promises

outline.title: Excuse 5: I Am Too Ashamed to Pray
subpoints: Adam and Eve hid from God in shame — but God was the one who sought them out
God already knows everything; hiding from him only adds prayerlessness to the shame
Go to God precisely because of shame: He can forgive, cleanse, and restore
Continued hiding hardens the heart; confession is always the better path

outline.title: Excuse 6: I Am Content with Mediocrity
subpoints: Contentment with outward Sunday religion while neglecting private prayer and Bible reading
James 4:1-2: you do not have because you do not ask
When prayer is selfish in motive, it is also unanswered (James 4:3)
The cure: repentance — submit to God, draw near to God, and he will draw near to you (James 4:7-10)

summary:
This lesson works through six common excuses for prayerlessness, drawn from a study text on praying with Paul. Each excuse is examined sympathetically — the teacher acknowledges that all of them are familiar from personal experience — but each is ultimately shown to be without biblical warrant.

The first excuse is busyness, which Martin Luther famously inverted: the more demanding the day, the more essential the time spent in prayer before it. The story of Mary and Martha is the key text, where Jesus gently rebukes Martha’s frantic activity and affirms that Mary, sitting at his feet, has chosen the better thing. A comic illustration of a motorist who refuses to stop for gas, then runs out again, captures how Christians rush through life without stopping to refuel spiritually.

The second through fifth excuses move from the pragmatic to the deeply personal: feeling too spiritually dry, feeling no need, being too bitter, and being too ashamed. In each case the teacher argues that the excuse actually inverts the proper response. Spiritual dryness is precisely why one should pray, not a reason to abstain. Feeling no need is a symptom of dangerous self-sufficiency, as illustrated by Joshua’s blunder with the Gibeonites and Hezekiah’s pride. Bitterness corrupts prayer at its root by preventing the honest confession of sin that real prayer demands. Shame, far from being an obstacle, is a summons — God sought out ashamed Adam and Eve, and he seeks us out as well.

The final excuse, contentment with mediocrity, is perhaps the most subtle: a form of outward religion that maintains appearances without the engine of private prayer. James 4 is the defining text — you do not have because you do not ask, and when you do ask it is with wrong motives. The remedy is not trying harder but repentance and a genuine drawing near to God.
transcript:
## Introduction

All right, we’re continuing our study on praying with Paul and we come to the chapter which is the convicting chapter — the chapter on excuses why we don’t pray. I think most of the book has been convicting so far. So let’s just say another word of prayer before we get started on this. Lord, we thank you again that we can pray and thank you that we have your word and we have really good books that explain your word to us and that’s really good. And tonight, maybe you can make us run to prayer and not away from it. That’s a surprising thing to ask. So there are several excuses — you’ll think of more as you meditate on this later.

## Excuse 1: I’m Too Busy to Pray

The first excuse I’m going to talk about is the most overused and lamest excuse that we all use all the time — that it’s too busy to pray. And just thinking about that excuse makes me think of more and more of the same. Martin Luther was so busy, but he had to spend a lot of time in prayer before he went about his busy day. That’s basically the opposite of how we treat prayer most of the time, I think.

We live in a busy age, right? We are always on the go. We’re always doing something. We’re really good at filling up our calendar with things. Everything’s kind of fast-paced these days — fast food is everywhere. And when we stop and actually have a minute to think and breathe, we don’t stop to think and breathe. We don’t stop to contemplate. We just stop to absorb whatever happens to be in front of us at the time — whether that’s just turning on the TV or a computer screen or a phone. And so the result of this is that we seldom actually sit down to think and reflect. We don’t like to have quiet, prayerful time. That’s really what it comes down to.

There was a funny story that someone told years ago about him and his wife. They’re driving somewhere and they see someone on the side of the road, broken down. So they stop and they ask the guy what’s wrong and he’s distraught. He’s trying to get somewhere and he ran out of gas. So they gave him some gas — enough to get him to the service station just down the road. And then they continued on their journey. About 10-12 miles down the road, they see the same guy broken down again. He didn’t stop at the service station. He just ran out of gas again. And that’s exactly what we do every day when we don’t pray.

So what does the Bible actually say about this excuse? Think about the story of Mary and Martha in Luke 10. Martha is so busy when Jesus arrives. There’s all these guests, and Jesus the Son of God is there. So Martha is really busy making preparations. And her sister Mary is just sitting at Jesus’ feet, just being with him. Not worrying about whatever food has to be made. And so Martha is really agitated to the point where she finally goes up to Jesus and says, Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her to help me. And what does Jesus say? He says in verse 41, Martha, Martha, you are worried and troubled about many things. But one thing is needed, and Mary has chosen that good part which will not be taken away from her. That’s a pretty convicting lesson right there for us. If we’re really too busy to pray, then that means something else needs to be cut. We need to make prayer a priority.

## Excuse 2: I Feel Too Spiritually Dry to Pray

The second excuse that we often make is I just feel too spiritually dry to pray. I just don’t feel like praying. And we’ve all been there, right? Sometimes we’re just tired. We just don’t feel like we have the energy. Sometimes maybe someone’s hurt us and we don’t want to pray. Maybe we’re in a pessimistic state of mind, or we’re overstressed emotionally. Whatever the case may be, we feel like the mountain is too high to climb.

But there are two really horrible presuppositions at work behind this attitude. The first one is the assumption that my approach to God should be tied to how I feel. We act like that’s a valid excuse. But is God impressed with us when we feel good about ourselves? Is it our feelings that make our approach to God valuable? Or is it the mediator, the office of the Son of God, appearing before the Father? We have to preach to ourselves. We have to remind ourselves that God does not hear us because we feel righteous or because we’re in a good mood. We can’t give up on prayer because we just don’t feel like it.

The second presupposition behind this is that our obligation to pray somehow diminishes when we don’t feel up to it. That’s a very self-centered attitude. It makes my mood the deciding factor for whether I obey God.

So what does the Bible say about this? There are two parables. The first is the parable of the persistent widow. There’s this judge who is unjust, who neither feared God nor cared what people thought. And the widow comes to him repeatedly, pleading her case. And eventually he decides to vindicate her, not because he cares, but because her continual coming wears him out. Now, the lesson here is not that God is an unjust judge. The lesson is that we need to be persistent in prayer. Because sometimes God wants us to grow in faith, and that’s why he doesn’t immediately answer. The question Jesus is answering is: do people have faith? Do we have the faith to persist in prayer, whether we feel like it or not?

The second parable is the friend at midnight who goes to his neighbor to get some bread for guests who have arrived unexpectedly. The neighbor is already in bed and says, go away. But because of the man’s persistence, he gets up and gives him what he needs. Jesus says in verse 9: ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you. Those who keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking — those are the ones who find. The ones who are stressed out and pray anyway. Those who are hurting and pray anyway.

## Excuse 3: I Feel No Need to Pray

The third excuse is: I feel no need to pray. Now I don’t think most of us would make this excuse out loud. We would probably not admit, I don’t feel like I need to pray. But we do act like it sometimes. Sometimes the reality is that we don’t pray because we just feel confident in ourselves. We feel like we’ve got everything worked out. We’ve strategically acknowledged the need to pray, but in practice we only really think that prayer is more important for other people — people who are weaker, people who are going through really difficult times.

Let’s look at an example of the consequences when we decide that we are not going to pray. Joshua and the Gibeonites. Joshua has led Israel into the Promised Land and they’ve seen God’s power in the destruction of Jericho and the crossing of the Jordan. At this point the Gibeonites approach Israel, put on old clothes, take dilapidated sandals and mouldy bread, and come pretending to be from a distant land. And Joshua 9:14-15 says that the men of Israel took some of their provisions, but they did not ask counsel of the Lord. So Joshua made peace with them and made a covenant with them to let them live. And the consequences were not good, because the Gibeonites were not from a distant land.

This is what we do. We see an opportunity in front of us — maybe a job opportunity — and we take it. We don’t even pray about it. We think we’re spiritually mature, we’re intelligent, we’ve got all the facts in front of us. We don’t even consult the Lord. Another example is Hezekiah. He prayed for 15 more years of life, and God granted that prayer. But when the envoy from Babylon came, Hezekiah was very flattered by them and decided to show them the wealth of the nation. And 2 Chronicles 32:31 says that God withdrew from him in order to test him, that he might know all that was in his heart. And what was found was overweening pride.

The worst thing that God could do to you when you’re not praying or living in some kind of sin — what’s the worst thing that God could do? Abandon you. Just let you keep going down that road.

## Excuse 4: I Am Too Bitter to Pray

The next excuse: I am too bitter to pray. When injustice is directed at us personally, it’s hard to deal with. And sometimes we find it so hard that we just stop praying. We might be tempted toward revenge, bitterness, malice, gossip. And holding those attitudes in our hearts immediately affects our prayer life in negative ways. Maybe it just makes our prayers more formulaic than they should be. We’re bitter, we’re angry, we know we need to pray so we go and do it, but there’s no heart in it. But eventually, if that bitterness takes root, it leads us entirely away from prayer.

Why is this? Because genuine prayer forces us to acknowledge and deal with our own sin. It forces us to confess our sin, and to repent, rather than cherishing it. There will be no real prayer unless that happens first. It’s very hard — probably impossible — to pray with zeal and fervor and sincerity when we’re harboring bitterness in our hearts.

Matthew 6:14-15 says, for if you forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you. But if you do not forgive men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses. And Mark 11:25 says, and whenever you stand praying, if you have anything against anyone, forgive him, that your Father in heaven may also forgive your trespasses. So what’s the point of these two passages? Is it that when I forgive someone who’s hurt me, I earn God’s forgiveness? No. The point is that when we actually forgive others, it shows that we ourselves are sincere in our desire to be forgiven by God. It shows that we genuinely desire to be forgiven because we ourselves have extended forgiveness to those who have hurt us.

Ephesians 4:31-32: let all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamor and evil speaking be put away from you, with all malice. And be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you. If a perfect and holy God who is without sin has forgiven you every sin that has ever come out of your life, you can forgive your brother for the wrong done to you.

## Excuse 5: I Am Too Ashamed to Pray

The next excuse: I am too ashamed to pray. What was Adam and Eve’s response to God after they sinned in the garden? They hid themselves. Who was it that came looking for them? It was God who came looking for them. So they were ashamed and they went and hid. God came to them and sought them out. He called them. He found them. And they responded when he asked, what is going on here?

The lesson here is that God is the one who sought them when they were ashamed. We can’t hide ourselves from God. And we need to remember that number one, he is the one who sought us out to deal with our sin. And he already knows what happened anyway. Proverbs 5:21 says, for the ways of man are before the eyes of the Lord and he ponders all their paths. And Hebrews 4:13 says there is no creature hidden from his sight, but all things are naked and open to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.

If we feel too ashamed to pray, then we need to pray. Don’t let shame keep you from prayer. Rather, go to God — he already knows, and he can forgive you and cleanse you and give you the freedom and fullness to pray again. Also remember that if shame drives you away from God and you stop praying, nothing good is going to happen after that. The worst thing you can do is keep hiding from God. The best thing — the absolute best thing — is to get on your knees and pray.

## Excuse 6: I Am Content with Mediocrity

The last excuse we’ll cover tonight is another one that we’re not going to admit. And that is: I am content with mediocrity. Sometimes we’re just content with an outward show of religion. We’re content to show up on Sunday. We’re perfectly fine if we appear to be good people. We don’t really care if our heart is changed. We might be really concerned with the preacher’s sermon. We might fret over appearing morally upright to everybody. We might even pray on Wednesday nights and try to make it sound like we’re really godly. But at the end of the day, we don’t engage in private prayer. We don’t read our Bibles. Because all we care about is our reputation. We’re content with being mediocre. We’re content with being Sunday Christians.

James 4:1-2 says this: where do wars and fights come from among you? Do they not come from your desires for pleasure that war in your members? You lust and do not have. You murder and covet and cannot obtain. You fight and war. Yet you do not have because you do not ask. So James is addressing Christians who are fighting amongst themselves, frustrated with each other, and they don’t get the things that they want because they don’t even ask for them. They don’t pray. And when they do pray, they’re praying for self-indulgent desires. Verse 3 says: you ask and do not receive, because you ask amiss, that you may spend it on your pleasures.

Thankfully, we have a cure in verses 7 through 10. James says, therefore, submit to God. Resist the devil and he will flee from you. Draw near to God and he will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners, and purify your hearts, you double-minded. Lament and mourn and weep. Let your laughter be turned to mourning and your joy to gloom. Humble yourselves in the sight of the Lord, and he will lift you up. The cure for being a Sunday Christian is repentance, seeking the Lord. And if you don’t feel like you’re being sincere, the best thing you can do is just go and ask the Lord to change your heart. And don’t stop until he does.

## Conclusion

Sadly, all of these excuses, I think, all of us make from time to time to one degree or another. And so we need to think about these things and more importantly do something about these things and actually commit ourselves to pray.

So I’ll leave you with a few questions to reflect on. Are there other excuses that you make? Every excuse not to pray is not valid. Do you ever appeal to any of these excuses to justify your prayerlessness? Again, not valid. And lastly, remember and think about this: does God accept us because of what we do or how we feel? Or does he accept us because of his Son? It’s always because of his Son. There’s never an excuse not to pray.

Let’s pray. Father, thank you for your word. We thank you for your goodness to us and your mercy to us. We thank you that we can, indeed, because of Jesus Christ, come to you in prayer. We pray that you would convict us in the areas where we fail. That you would encourage us and help us repent of our prayerlessness, and we ask you to bless us as we go. In the name of Jesus Christ, amen.
